vulnerability.title: Disable core dump backtraces
vulnerability.description: The ProcessSizeMax option in [Coredump] section of /etc/systemd/coredump.conf or in a drop-in file under /etc/systemd/coredump.conf.d/ specifies the maximum size in bytes of a core which will be processed. Core dumps exceeding this size may be stored, but the backtrace will not be generated.
vulnerability.recommendation:
Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q systemd; }; then found=false # set value in all files if they contain section or key for f in $(echo -n "/etc/systemd/coredump.conf.d/complianceascode_hardening.conf /etc/systemd/coredump.conf.d/*.conf /etc/systemd/coredump.conf"); do if [ ! -e "$f" ]; then continue fi # find key in section and change value if grep -qzosP "[[:space:]]*\[Coredump\]([^
\[]*
+)+?[[:space:]]*ProcessSizeMax" "$f"; then if ! grep -qPz "ProcessSizeMax=0" "$f"; then sed -i "s/ProcessSizeMax[^(
)]*/ProcessSizeMax=0/" "$f" fi found=true # find section and add key = value to it elif grep -qs "[[:space:]]*\[Coredump\]" "$f"; then sed -i "/[[:space:]]*\[Coredump\]/a ProcessSizeMax=0" "$f" found=true fi done # if s

triage_decision.reason: Setting ProcessSizeMax=0 in /etc/systemd/coredump.conf or its drop‑in files disables backtrace generation for core dumps, a standard hardening step that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services.

previous_attempts.scan_output: Single-rule scan failed for coredump_disable_backtraces (rule=xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_rule_coredump_disable_backtraces). Could not verify.

llm_verdict.message:
I couldn’t execute any remediation commands because the SSH session closed immediately after each attempt:

```
Connection closed by 192.168.1.221 port 22
```

Without an active connection I can’t read or edit `/etc/systemd/coredump.conf` or the drop‑in files, so I’m unable to apply the required `ProcessSizeMax=0` setting.  

Please verify that the SSH daemon is running and that you can establish a stable connection again. Once the connection is restored, let me know and I’ll resume the steps:

1. Read the relevant `coredump.conf` files.  
2. Remove any existing `ProcessSizeMax` line.  
3. Add `ProcessSizeMax=0` under a `[Coredump]` section (creating the section if it doesn’t exist).  
4. Run `systemctl daemon-reload` to apply the changes.

Thank you.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan will disable core‑dump backtraces by setting ProcessSizeMax=0 as required, but it omits a systemd daemon reload and does not ensure a [Coredump] section if missing.
concerns: No systemctl daemon-reload after editing the configuration files.
If a target file lacks a [Coredump] section, the added ProcessSizeMax=0 line may not be applied properly.
suggested_improvements: After completing all sed changes, run `systemctl daemon-reload` to make the changes effective.

If a file does not contain a [Coredump] section, create the section before appending ProcessSizeMax=0, e.g. `sed -i '/\[Coredump\]/a ProcessSizeMax=0'` or add `[Coredump]
ProcessSizeMax=0` at the end.
Make a backup of each configuration file before editing (e.g., `cp -p file file.bak`).
Use grub or systemd to ensure proper ownership and permissions remain unchanged.

concerns: The plan only modifies /etc/systemd/coredump.conf and ignores any drop‑in files under /etc/systemd/coredump.conf.d/, which can override the main configuration. If such drop‑ins exist and set a larger ProcessSizeMax value, the vulnerability would remain unaddressed.
suggested_improvements: Extend the script to search for and edit any existing drop‑in files that contain a [Coredump] section, ensuring ProcessSizeMax=0 is set there as well.
After editing, verify that file ownership and permissions remain unchanged (e.g., by using --preserve=mode,ownership in cp or by resetting them explicitly).
